Introduction to Computer Vision: Plant Seedlings Classification¶

Problem Statement¶

Context¶

In recent times, the field of agriculture has been in urgent need of modernizing, since the amount of manual work people need to put in to check if plants are growing correctly is still highly extensive. Despite several advances in agricultural technology, people working in the agricultural industry still need to have the ability to sort and recognize different plants and weeds, which takes a lot of time and effort in the long term. The potential is ripe for this trillion-dollar industry to be greatly impacted by technological innovations that cut down on the requirement for manual labor, and this is where Artificial Intelligence can actually benefit the workers in this field, as the time and energy required to identify plant seedlings will be greatly shortened by the use of AI and Deep Learning. The ability to do so far more efficiently and even more effectively than experienced manual labor, could lead to better crop yields, the freeing up of human inolvement for higher-order agricultural decision making, and in the long term will result in more sustainable environmental practices in agriculture as well.

Objective¶

The aim of this project is to Build a Convolutional Neural Netowrk to classify plant seedlings into their respective categories.

Data Dictionary¶

The Aarhus University Signal Processing group, in collaboration with the University of Southern Denmark, has recently released a dataset containing images of unique plants belonging to 12 different species.

  • The dataset can be download from Olympus.
  • The data file names are:
    • images.npy
    • Labels.csv

  • Due to the large volume of data, the images were converted to the images.npy file and the labels are also put into Labels.csv, so that you can work on the data/project seamlessly without having to worry about the high data volume.

  • The goal of the project is to create a classifier capable of determining a plant's species from an image.

List of Species

  • Black-grass
  • Charlock
  • Cleavers
  • Common Chickweed
  • Common Wheat
  • Fat Hen
  • Loose Silky-bent
  • Maize
  • Scentless Mayweed
  • Shepherds Purse
  • Small-flowered Cranesbill
  • Sugar beet

Note: Please use GPU runtime on Google Colab to execute the code faster.¶

Importing necessary libraries¶

In [1]:
# Installing the libraries with the specified version.
!pip install tensorflow==2.15.0 scikit-learn==1.2.2 seaborn==0.13.1 matplotlib==3.7.1 numpy==1.25.2 pandas==2.0.3 opencv-python==4.8.0.76 -q --user

Note: After running the above cell, kindly restart the notebook kernel and run all cells sequentially from the start again.

In [2]:
import math
import os
import random
import warnings

import cv2
from google.colab.patches import cv2_imshow

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Scikit-learn modules
from sklearn import preprocessing
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelBinarizer

# TensorFlow modules
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import backend
from tensorflow.keras.layers import BatchNormalization, Conv2D, Dense, Dropout, Flatten, MaxPooling2D
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import Adam, SGD
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator

# Keras callbacks
from keras.callbacks import ReduceLROnPlateau

# Ignore warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

Loading the dataset¶

In [4]:
# Get data from gdrive
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Mounted at /content/drive
In [5]:
# Paths for data
# images_data_path = "/content/drive/MyDrive/AIML-Certification/AIML-training/Computer Vision/Wk2-Trasnsfer Learning/Project- plant species/images.npy"
# labels_data_path = "/content/drive/MyDrive/AIML-Certification/AIML-training/Computer Vision/Wk2-Trasnsfer Learning/Project- plant species/Labels.csv"

images_data_path = "/content/drive/MyDrive/aiml/images.npy"
labels_data_path = "/content/drive/MyDrive/aiml/Labels.csv"
In [6]:
#images data to np array
images = np.load(images_data_path)
In [7]:
#labels data to np array
labels = pd.read_csv(labels_data_path)

Data Overview¶

Understand the shape of the dataset¶

In [8]:
# images data shape
images.shape
Out[8]:
(4750, 128, 128, 3)
In [9]:
# labels data shape
labels.shape
Out[9]:
(4750, 1)

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you understand the data better.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.
  1. How are these different category plant images different from each other?
  2. Is the dataset provided an imbalance? (Check with using bar plots)
In [10]:
# Common function to plot or visualize images
def plot_images(images,labels):
  num_classes=10
  categories=np.unique(labels)
  keys=dict(labels['Label'])
  rows = 3
  cols = 4
  fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
  for i in range(cols):
      for j in range(rows):
          random_index = np.random.randint(0, len(labels))
          ax = fig.add_subplot(rows, cols, i * rows + j + 1)
          ax.imshow(images[random_index, :])
          ax.set_title(keys[random_index])
  plt.show()
In [11]:
plot_images(images, labels)
In [12]:
labels.info()

Data Pre-Processing¶

Convert the BGR images to RGB images.¶

In [14]:
for image in range(len(images)):
  images[image] = cv2.cvtColor(images[image], cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)

Resize the images¶

As the size of the images is large, it may be computationally expensive to train on these larger images; therefore, it is preferable to reduce the image size from 128 to 64.

In [15]:
# Decrease image size
images_decreased=[]
height = 64
width =  64
dimensions = (width, height)
for i in range(len(images)):
  images_decreased.append( cv2.resize(images[i], dimensions, interpolation=cv2.INTER_LINEAR))
In [16]:
# before resizing
plt.imshow(images[3])
Out[16]:
<matplotlib.image.AxesImage at 0x790d8a0d17b0>
In [17]:
# After resizing
plt.imshow(images_decreased[3])
Out[17]:
<matplotlib.image.AxesImage at 0x790d89fd02b0>

Data Preparation for Modeling¶

  • Before you proceed to build a model, you need to split the data into train, test, and validation to be able to evaluate the model that you build on the train data
  • You'll have to encode categorical features and scale the pixel values.
  • You will build a model using the train data and then check its performance

Split the dataset

In [18]:
X_temp, X_test, y_temp, y_test = train_test_split(np.array(images_decreased),labels, test_size=0.1, random_state=42, stratify=labels)
In [19]:
y_temp.shape, y_test.shape, X_temp.shape, X_test.shape
Out[19]:
((4275, 1), (475, 1), (4275, 64, 64, 3), (475, 64, 64, 3))
In [20]:
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(X_temp, y_temp, test_size=0.2, random_state=42, stratify=y_temp)
In [21]:
#check shapes of train and test and validation data sets
print(X_train.shape,y_train.shape)
print(X_val.shape,y_val.shape)
print(X_test.shape,y_test.shape)

(3420, 64, 64, 3) (3420, 1)
(855, 64, 64, 3) (855, 1)
(475, 64, 64, 3) (475, 1)

Encode the target labels¶

In [22]:
# encoding the lables as onehot encoding
label_binarizer = LabelBinarizer()
In [23]:
y_train_encoded = label_binarizer.fit_transform(y_train)
In [24]:
y_val_encoded=label_binarizer.transform(y_val)
In [25]:
y_test_encoded=label_binarizer.transform(y_test)
In [26]:
y_train_encoded.shape,y_val_encoded.shape,y_test_encoded.shape
Out[26]:
((3420, 12), (855, 12), (475, 12))

Data Normalization¶

In [27]:
X_train_normalized = X_train.astype('float32')/255.0
X_val_normalized = X_val.astype('float32')/255.0
X_test_normalized = X_test.astype('float32')/255.0
In [28]:
X_train_normalized.shape, X_val_normalized.shape, X_test_normalized.shape
Out[28]:
((3420, 64, 64, 3), (855, 64, 64, 3), (475, 64, 64, 3))

Model Building¶

In [29]:
# clear session
backend.clear_session()
In [30]:
# setup for consistent randomness
np.random.seed(42)
random.seed(42)
tf.random.set_seed(42)
In [31]:
# Get sequential model
model1 = Sequential()

# 1st c layer with 128 filters use 'same' for padding
model1.add(Conv2D(128, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same", input_shape=(64, 64, 3)))

# 2nd layer
model1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding = 'same'))

# 3rd layer
model1.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding = 'same'))

# 4th layer
model1.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding = 'same'))

# add flatten layer
model1.add(Flatten())

# fully connected layer with 16 neurons
model1.add(Dense(16, activation='relu'))
model1.add(Dropout(0.3))

#out layer with 12 neurons
model1.add(Dense(12, activation='softmax'))

# compile the model using adam optimizer and loss function cate crossentropy
opt=Adam(lr=0.001)
model1.compile(optimizer=opt, loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# model summary
model1.summary()

WARNING:absl:`lr` is deprecated in Keras optimizer, please use `learning_rate` or use the legacy optimizer, e.g.,tf.keras.optimizers.legacy.Adam.

X_train_normalized.shape, y_train_encoded.shape, X_val_normalized.shape, y_val_encoded.shape
Out[32]:
((3420, 64, 64, 3), (3420, 12), (855, 64, 64, 3), (855, 12))
In [33]:
# check the sizes of train and validate datasets
print("X_train_normalized shape:", X_train_normalized.shape)
print("y_train_encoded shape:", y_train_encoded.shape)
print("X_val_normalized shape:", X_val_normalized.shape)
print("y_val_encoded shape:", y_val_encoded.shape)

X_train_normalized shape: (3420, 64, 64, 3)
y_train_encoded shape: (3420, 12)
X_val_normalized shape: (855, 64, 64, 3)
y_val_encoded shape: (855, 12)
In [34]:
# fit model1 with increased epochs and batchsize
history_1 = model1.fit(
            X_train_normalized, y_train_encoded,
            epochs=90,
            validation_data=(X_val_normalized,y_val_encoded),
            batch_size=32,
            verbose=2
)

Model Performance Improvement¶

Reducing the Learning Rate:

Hint: Use ReduceLRonPlateau() function that will be used to decrease the learning rate by some factor, if the loss is not decreasing for some time. This may start decreasing the loss at a smaller learning rate. There is a possibility that the loss may still not decrease. This may lead to executing the learning rate reduction again in an attempt to achieve a lower loss.

In [35]:
learning_rate_reduction = ReduceLROnPlateau(monitor='val_accuracy',
                                            patience=3,
                                            verbose=1,
                                            factor=0.5,
                                            min_lr=0.00001)
In [36]:
# Clear session
from tensorflow.keras import backend
backend.clear_session()

# set the consistent randomness
import random
np.random.seed(42)
random.seed(42)
tf.random.set_seed(42)

train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
                              rotation_range=20,
                              fill_mode='nearest'
                              )
In [37]:
# Setting sequential model
model2 = Sequential()

# 1st conv layer with 32 filters, using 'same' padding
model2.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same", input_shape=(64, 64, 3)))
model2.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

# 2nd c layer with 64 filters
model2.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model2.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

# 3rd c layer with 64 filters
model2.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model2.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

model2.add(Flatten())

# 32 neurons fully connected layer
model2.add(Dense(32, activation='relu'))
model2.add(Dropout(0.3))

# output layer with 12 neurons using softmax activation
model2.add(Dense(12, activation='softmax'))

#  model compile using Adam optimizer and loss function categorical crossentropy
model2.compile(optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.001), loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Model summary
model2.summary()

Model: "sequential"

 max_pooling2d_1 (MaxPoolin  (None, 16, 16, 64)        0         
 g2D)

 g2D)

_________________________________________________________________
In [38]:
# model2 for different trianing params
# Clearing session
from tensorflow.keras import backend
backend.clear_session()

# common variables set for consistent randomness
import random
np.random.seed(42)
random.seed(42)
tf.random.set_seed(42)

train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
                              rotation_range=20,
                              fill_mode='nearest'
                              )
# start sequential model
model2_1 = Sequential()

# first conv layer with 32 filters and kernel size 3x3, using 'same' padding for input and output size sync
model2_1.add(Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same", input_shape=(64, 64, 3)))
model2_1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

# 2nd conv layer with 64 filters
model2_1.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model2_1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

# 3rd conv layer with 64 filters
model2_1.add(Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu', padding="same"))
model2_1.add(MaxPooling2D((2, 2), padding='same'))

# output flattening
model2_1.add(Flatten())

# fully connected layer with 32 neurons
model2_1.add(Dense(32, activation='relu'))
model2_1.add(Dropout(0.3))

# softmax activation and adjusting 12 neurons
model2_1.add(Dense(12, activation='softmax'))

# model compile using Adam optimizer and categorical crossentropy loss function
model2_1.compile(optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.001), loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Model summary
model2_1.summary()

Model: "sequential"
_________________________________________________________________
 Layer (type)                Output Shape              Param #   
=================================================================
 conv2d (Conv2D)             (None, 64, 64, 32)        896       
                                                                 
 max_pooling2d (MaxPooling2  (None, 32, 32, 32)        0         
 D)                                                              
                                                                 
 conv2d_1 (Conv2D)           (None, 32, 32, 64)        18496     
                                                                 
 max_pooling2d_1 (MaxPoolin  (None, 16, 16, 64)        0         
 g2D)                                                            
                                                                 
 conv2d_2 (Conv2D)           (None, 16, 16, 64)        36928     
                                                                 
 max_pooling2d_2 (MaxPoolin  (None, 8, 8, 64)          0         
 g2D)                                                            
                                                                 
 flatten (Flatten)           (None, 4096)              0         
                                                                 
 dense (Dense)               (None, 32)                131104    
                                                                 
 dropout (Dropout)           (None, 32)                0         
                                                                 
 dense_1 (Dense)             (None, 12)                396       
                                                                 
=================================================================

Trainable params: 187820 (733.67 KB)
Non-trainable params: 0 (0.00 Byte)
_________________________________________________________________
In [39]:
# check the model2 performance with 10 epochs
history_2 = model2.fit(train_datagen.flow(X_train_normalized,y_train_encoded,
                                       batch_size=10,
                                       shuffle=False),
                                       epochs=5,
                                       steps_per_epoch=X_train_normalized.shape[0] // 10,
                                       validation_data=(X_val_normalized,y_val_encoded),
                                       verbose=1,callbacks=[learning_rate_reduction])

Epoch 1/5

# check the model2 performance with 10 epochs

                                       batch_size=10,

                                       steps_per_epoch=X_train_normalized.shape[0] // 10,

# check the model2 performance with 25 epochs

                                       batch_size=70,
                                       shuffle=False),
                                       epochs=25,
                                       steps_per_epoch=X_train_normalized.shape[0] // 70,

                                       batch_size=70,

As going with more epochs the accuracy and loss has good improvement

Model evaluation¶

In [43]:
# model1 performance graph
plt.plot(history_1.history['accuracy'])
plt.plot(history_1.history['val_accuracy'])
plt.title('Model Accuracy')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.xlabel('Epoch')
plt.legend(['Train', 'Validation'], loc='upper left')
plt.show()

the train and validation accuracy graph seems very nice as epochs increases, but it seems like a overfit model, lets see how it performs with test data

In [44]:
# history_2 plot model accuracy with train and validation dataset
plt.plot(history_2.history['accuracy'])
plt.plot(history_2.history['val_accuracy'])
plt.title('Model Accuracy')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.xlabel('Epoch')
plt.legend(['Train', 'Validation'], loc='upper left')
plt.show()

As epochs increases the Accuracy has a good progress for val and train data

In [64]:
# history_2_1 plot model accuracy with train and validation dataset
plt.plot(history_2_1.history['accuracy'])
plt.plot(history_2_1.history['val_accuracy'])
plt.title('Model Accu')
plt.ylabel('Accu')
plt.xlabel('Epoch')
plt.legend(['Train', 'Validation'], loc='upper left')
plt.show()

It looks good on both train and validation and we will more context upon chekcing it with test data

Model on test data¶

In [46]:
# get the model outputs against test data
accuracy1 = model1.evaluate(X_test, y_test_encoded, verbose=2)

15/15 - 0s - loss: 1539.4976 - accuracy: 0.4021 - 462ms/epoch - 31ms/step
In [47]:
# get the model outputs against test data
accuracy2 = model2.evaluate(X_test, y_test_encoded, verbose=2)

15/15 - 0s - loss: 924.7999 - accuracy: 0.3579 - 366ms/epoch - 24ms/step
In [48]:
# get the model outputs against test data
accuracy2_1 = model2_1.evaluate(X_test, y_test_encoded, verbose=2)

15/15 - 0s - loss: 145.6323 - accuracy: 0.5326 - 293ms/epoch - 20ms/step

Confusion Matrix¶

In [49]:
# setting the ground for confusion matrix
y_pred1=model1.predict(X_test_normalized)

15/15 [==============================] - 0s 4ms/step
In [50]:
y_pred2=model2.predict(X_test_normalized)

15/15 [==============================] - 0s 2ms/step
In [51]:
# this one ran after the above one
y_pred2_1 = model2_1.predict(X_test_normalized)

15/15 [==============================] - 0s 3ms/step
In [52]:
# check the shapes for further validation
y_test.shape, y_test_encoded.shape, y_pred1.shape, y_pred2.shape, y_pred2_1.shape
Out[52]:
((475, 1), (475, 12), (475, 12), (475, 12), (475, 12))

Model1 confusion matrix¶

In [63]:
# Get categorical values from test and pred
y_pred1_arg=np.argmax(y_pred1,axis=1)
y_test_arg=np.argmax(y_test_encoded,axis=1)
print(f"y_pred- {y_pred1_arg.shape}, y_pred_arg- {y_test_arg.shape}")

confusion_matrix = tf.math.confusion_matrix(y_pred1_arg,y_test_arg)
f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 13))
sns.heatmap(
    confusion_matrix,
    annot=True,
    linewidths=.5,
    fmt="d",
    square=True,
    ax=ax
)
# labels to x and y axes
ax.set_xlabel('Predicted labels');ax.set_ylabel('True labels');
ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix');
ax.xaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=30)
ax.yaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=20)
plt.show()

y_pred- (475,), y_pred_arg- (475,)

Except loose silky bent others have good prediction values

Model2 confusion matrix¶

In [62]:
# Get categorical values from test_encoded and pred datasets
y_pred2_arg=np.argmax(y_pred2,axis=1)
y_test_arg=np.argmax(y_test_encoded,axis=1)
print(f"y_pred2- {y_pred2_arg.shape}, y_pred_arg- {y_test_arg.shape}")

confusion_matrix = tf.math.confusion_matrix(y_pred2_arg,y_test_arg)
f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 13))
sns.heatmap(
    confusion_matrix,
    annot=True,
    linewidths=.5,
    fmt="d",
    square=True,
    ax=ax
)
# set lables to x and y axes
ax.set_xlabel('Predicted labels');ax.set_ylabel('True labels');
ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix');
ax.xaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=30)
ax.yaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=20)
plt.show()

y_pred2- (475,), y_pred_arg- (475,)

Loose silky bent, common wheat has wrong predictions but other type of species has better result, may be we can apply more epoch for the training or we can apply a better value for dimesional reduction to get more accuracy

Model2_1 confusion matrix¶

In [61]:
# Get the categorical values from test and pred data
y_pred2_1_arg=np.argmax(y_pred2_1,axis=1)
y_test_arg=np.argmax(y_test_encoded,axis=1)
print(f"y_pred2- {y_pred2_1arg.shape}, y_pred_arg- {y_test_arg.shape}")

# Code for Confusion Matrix
confusion_matrix = tf.math.confusion_matrix(y_pred2_1_arg,y_test_arg)
f, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 13))
sns.heatmap(
    confusion_matrix,
    annot=True,
    linewidths=.5,
    fmt="d",
    square=True,
    ax=ax
)
# Set labels to x and y axes
ax.set_xlabel('Predicted labels');ax.set_ylabel('True labels');
ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix');
ax.xaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=30)
ax.yaxis.set_ticklabels(list(label_binarizer.classes_),rotation=20)
plt.show()

y_pred2- (475,), y_pred_arg- (475,)

This model looks good with epochs 125 and increased batch size but the loss and accuracy was not good

Final Model¶

Comment on the final model you have selected and use the same in the below code to visualize the image.

  • Except for one variant- remianing predictions are good with model1, which has 128 layers at convolutional layer and 64 later, finally 16 neurons at the end ,
  • The training with 90 epochs and 32 batch size has good progression rate in acheiving better accuracy and loss values for both validation and train data sets

Visualizing the prediction¶

In [58]:
# Visualizing the predicted and true label of images using test data
plt.figure(figsize=(3,3))
plt.imshow(X_test[2])
plt.show()
# Checking the model prediction vs actual output using a sample image
print('Predicted Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(model1.predict((X_test_normalized[6].reshape(1,64,64,3)))))
# using encoder getting the true label of the image
print('True Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(y_test_encoded)[6])

plt.figure(figsize=(3,3))
plt.imshow(X_test[33])
plt.show()
# Checking the model prediction vs actual output using a sample image
print('Predicted Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(model1.predict((X_test_normalized[11].reshape(1,64,64,3)))))
# using encoder getting the true label of the image
print('True Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(y_test_encoded)[11])

plt.figure(figsize=(3,3))
plt.imshow(X_test[59],)
plt.show()

# Checking the model prediction vs actual output using a sample image
print('Predicted Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(model1.predict((X_test_normalized[43].reshape(1,64,64,3)))))
# using encoder getting the true label of the image
print('True Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(y_test_encoded)[43])

plt.figure(figsize=(3,3))
plt.imshow(X_test[36])
plt.show()

# Checking the model prediction vs actual output using a sample image
print('Predicted Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(model1.predict((X_test_normalized[19].reshape(1,64,64,3)))))
# using encoder getting the true label of the image
print('True Label', label_binarizer.inverse_transform(y_test_encoded)[19])

1/1 [==============================] - 0s 31ms/step
Predicted Label ['Sugar beet']
True Label Sugar beet

1/1 [==============================] - 0s 57ms/step
Predicted Label ['Common Chickweed']
True Label Common Chickweed

1/1 [==============================] - 0s 66ms/step
Predicted Label ['Small-flowered Cranesbill']
True Label Small-flowered Cranesbill

1/1 [==============================] - 0s 42ms/step
Predicted Label ['Maize']
True Label Maize

Actionable Insights and Business Recommendations¶

  • The model1 has great prediction accuracy
  • It can be further improved by adding more epochs and more batch_sizes to the model
  • Even adding more epochs to less layered model has shown good improvement upto a point but it started to degrade the performance when epochs were increased
  • Overall adding more layers and increasing epochs with batch_size and training it with more data has significant impact over all on the final mode predictions
  • we can ease the training performance by adding more dimensionality reduction to the images so that weight of images will go down further and improves the over all performance and run time
In [ ]:

jupyter nbconvert --to html /PATH/TO/YOUR/NOTEBOOKFILE.ipynb
